author: potuz
content: hmmm, came back home and looking to do it,  it's a rather non-trivial change, we only look for terminal difficulties after we get a VALID response from the EL. Changing this is not so easy. The alternative of doing it even if the EL returns ACCEPTED is also not nice because of what I mentioned above that it would break the symmetry of SYNCING/ACCEPTED in our code. At this point I think I'd rather wait to see if Erigon would execute the TTD block here, given that from what Marek said, it's possible that Nethermind can do it.

author: ajsutton
content: Yeah I think sending fcu for something that isn’t the head is a bad idea. Fcu is supposed to be for communicating what the chain head is, not a hacky way to get the EL to execute something.

author: ajsutton
content: My suggestion would be that the EL should be required to actually execute blocks they receive via newPayload if they have the data required and haven’t yet received a fcu with a finalized block.

author: ajsutton
content: That’s essentially the same as sending an fcu in this case which is just to force the EL to execute a non canonical block. It’s time bounded so the optimisation to not execute comes back in once the chain is finalising, avoids the risk of the CL being unable to reorg correctly around the merge and doesn’t require any protocol changes.

author: ajsutton
content: Or at least doesn’t require any changes to the on the wire engine api protocol - it is obviously changing the requirements on EL for newPayload.

author: ajsutton
content: Given they have to execute for the canonical chain and have to be able to reorg and execute for fcu it shouldn’t be too big a deal.

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> But you do use FCU to execute side chains that are not yet resolved if/when forkchoice takes over. So if it's ACCEPTED (and this means semantically that all blocks are there) and has the weight to be head, running FCU at that point is normal behaviour and wouldn't fc poison because accepted means blocks are there (if that's the actual semantics people are using)

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> I'm not suggesting pre-empting a FCU on non-canonical weight branches but if attestations say so, it should be safe to do to an ACCEPTED (rather than SYNCING) branch during this merge window

author: ajsutton
content: If it becomes head yes. But it can’t become head because it can’t be imported. And we can’t even tell it would have become head because we can’t process any attestations for it until it’s imported.

author: ajsutton
content: We’d have to optimistically import and then we’re subject to fork choice poisoning unless there’s some way to differentiate “EL has the data but chooses not to execute” from “EL is missing the required data to execute this”

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> ACCEPTED and SYNCING are supposed to be semantically different for this precise level of precision

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> But not all clients respect the intended semantics

author: ajsutton
content: I didn’t think syncing was a valid response from newPayload at all but maybe I missed something.

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> It is

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/blob/main/src/engine/specification.md#specification

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> ACCEPTED was added specifically for clients to be able to say "this is a  well formed chain but it's not cryptoeconomically valuable enough for me to execute. Say FCU if you really want me to"

author: ajsutton
content: Ah I completely missed that detail. In which case yes we could allow optimistic import for ACCEPTED in teku. It’s a bit of work but shouldn’t be too bad. But we’d have to be absolutely certain the EL always gets the distinction right.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<dannyryan> I can't remember the status anymore. It was being incorrectly used in different ways but then there was a bit more conformance, I believe 

In most cases, the distinction between the two terms don't actually cause CL to do something different so I stopped pushing too hard for the semantic distinction. That said, I believe it made it into hive

author: ajsutton
content: I’d still say it’s simpler not to have the distinction and just disable the optimisation in the EL until finality.

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> Geth is fine to execute such branches. It's more expensive  in erigon but certainly possible. I think nethermind as well

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> It'd be essentially, insert and (internal simulation of) FCU for status, then a revert to prior head

author: ajsutton
content: Yeah that’s my thinking basically. And it means we could remove the distinction from the protocol and simplify things so this transition complexity doesn’t have to live on forever.

author: bridge-bot
content: <dannyryan> > <dannyryan>  (and geth does "shallow fork" execution.for within 128 blocks from head) (re @dannyryan: Geth is fine to execute such branches. It's more expensive  in erigon but certainly possible. I think nethermind as well)

author: ajsutton
content: Although I guess we could remove the distinction post merge as well which is where we’d land either way since we don’t want to change things if we don’t have to now.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<dannyryan> ACCEPTED isn't just for transition. It's to make clear the difference between what EL is actually doing

(Waiting for valuable enough to execute vs doesn't have the data locally)

I guess I figure this is valuable info and strange to drop

author: ajsutton
content: As far as I know no CL makes use of the distinction and this is the first case I’ve come across where there was even a vague use case for it.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<dannyryan> Id say it's much less than vague here

But I agree that my assumption that it was valuable to know has been so far incorrect

author: ajsutton
content: Oh yeah I can see how it would affect CL behaviour here but we only just discovered this corner case. 🙂

author: ajsutton
content: So to pull this together to something actionable.  Are EL clients able to execute non-canonical blocks received via `engine_newPayload` if they have the data available to do so up until the first FCU with a finalized block? By the specs this would mean you can return `SYNCING` to say you're still downloading ancestor blocks or world state, but can't just choose not to execute because its non-canonical and return `ACCEPTED`.  <@&688101493978562687> <@&836981603216654336>  ?  My understanding is that Geth already does this and Nethermind are planning to.

author: funnygiulio
content: Yes we already do that

author: funnygiulio
content: We dont return VALID though as we dont know how much the process takes but we are executing them, and mark them valid

author: ajsutton
content: So if the beacon node retries would it get a VALID on the second attempt (assuming processing had completed)?

author: funnygiulio
content: Yes it would

author: funnygiulio
content: Given that full execution is done

author: ajsutton
content: Ok that’s a good start. The node would get stuck until it retried but not for the full safe slots period

author: ajsutton
content: Would it be possible to make it wait and return valid or invalid if the merge hasn’t finalised yet or is that hard to do?

author: funnygiulio
content: Side note: We skip validation of downloaded side forks only up to a distance from the head of 32, but thats the length of an epoch

author: funnygiulio
content: Probably we could make it wait in all scenarios(not just the merge) and return VALID on first attempt but things have to be right

author: funnygiulio
content: First of all, few blocks to download, secondly, does not take much to execute and P2P networking needs to be fully performant without any fluctuation

author: funnygiulio
content: It may be tedious to do something like that

author: ajsutton
content: If you need to download blocks SYNCING is always the right response. But if you already have the required blocks it would be good to get valid straight away if it can be done in a reasonable time (in particular if it’s on a non canonical fork that’s only a couple of blocks long)

author: funnygiulio
content: Ah if we have the ancestor and all that comes before, then yes we return VALID

author: ajsutton
content: Even for non canonical blocks? That’s quite different to what everyone seems to think Erigon will do.

author: funnygiulio
content: Yes also for side forks

author: funnygiulio
content: We use in-memory execution

author: funnygiulio
content: It is going to be in the next alpha release

author: funnygiulio
content: Erigon by design was not meant for concurrent forks, but I was able to hack a clean way to do it without risking major database corruptions

author: ajsutton
content: Right makes sense. I think that will make Erigon a much better client post merge as it will make reorgs cheaper , including for the CL as it shouldn’t have to go into optimistic mode or wait for the fcu response.

author: funnygiulio
content: I can actually try to make it return VALID on the first fcu in  case the above conditions are met with relative ease, would that help?

author: ajsutton
content: There won’t be a fcu in the case we’re worried about because the CL can’t optimistically import the block. We’d need to get VALID on the engine_newPayload call to allow the CL to import the block.

author: funnygiulio
content: Okay it can be done on the first newPayload as well, would that help?

author: ajsutton
content: Yeah I think it would help - especially right after the transition block.

author: potuz
content: In our case this would be all that's needed, we will only send the first newPayload on the merge block

author: funnygiulio
content: https://github.com/ledgerwatch/erigon/pull/4812 the PR is ready, it will come out with the next release

author: potuz
content:
Can you check the bitwise arithmetic cause it's dangerous? I think your test is not really covering the function
https://go.dev/play/p/JoBQvXyk1qf

author: potuz
content: The function is right though

author: potuz
content:
By the way, I'm convinced the compiled assembly will be faster with something like
```go
if x > y {
    return x - y
}
return y - x
```
At the very least I'm absolutely certain that in C++ `(x >= y) ? x - y : y - x`  will be the fastest compiled assembly. And your fellow reviewers will probably thank you for something easier to read than fancy xors 🙂

content: Sometimes i just like to have my fun :)

author: funnygiulio
